coredata.dc:title: A novel silkworm excrement-derived nanomedicine integrating ferroptosis and photodynamic therapy, well-suitable for PD-L1-mediated immune checkpoint blockade

coredata.dc:description: The inherent tumor immunosuppressive microenvironment (TIME) of most solid tumors adversely affect the antitumor efficacy of immune checkpoint blockade (ICB) therapy, which is an urgent issue to be solved in clinical cancer therapy. In this study, a novel red-emitting carbon dots (SCDs) with photodynamic therapy (PDT) function was prepared for the first time using silkworm excrement, a biological waste. Additionally, an original tumor-targeted nano-micelle (HA-Fc) was first developed by conjugating hyaluronic acid (HA) to ferrocene (Fc) via the acid-sensitive Schiff base, which were then used to encapsulate Cu-doped SCDs to obtain HF-SCDs@Cu. HF-SCDs@Cu exhibited the pronounced targeting propensity to tumor tissue and degraded to release ferrocene, Cu2+, and SCDs under the specific acidic tumor microenvironment, thereby eliciting potent tumor immunogenic cell death function induced by ferroptosis and PDT. Moreover, the O2-generating ability induced by HF-SCDs@Cu could reverse the hypoxic tumor microenvironment, enhancing the efficacy of PDT, and importantly, the expression of PD-L1 on tumor cell surfaces could be effectively downregulated by inhibiting the HIF-1α pathways, thereby augmenting the effect of anti-PD-L1 (αPD-L1) therapy. Therefore, this study presents a novel approach for the redevelopment of silkworm excrement and provides valuable strategies into enhancing PD-L1-mediated ICB therapy.

head.ce:title: A novel silkworm excrement-derived nanomedicine integrating ferroptosis and photodynamic therapy, well-suitable for PD-L1-mediated immune checkpoint blockade

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The inherent tumor immunosuppressive microenvironment (TIME) of most solid tumors adversely affect the antitumor efficacy of immune checkpoint blockade (ICB) therapy, which is an urgent issue to be solved in clinical cancer therapy. In this study, a novel red-emitting carbon dots (SCDs) with photodynamic therapy (PDT) function was prepared for the first time using silkworm excrement, a biological waste. Additionally, an original tumor-targeted nano-micelle (HA-Fc) was first developed by conjugating hyaluronic acid (HA) to ferrocene (Fc) via the acid-sensitive Schiff base, which were then used to encapsulate Cu-doped SCDs to obtain HF-SCDs@Cu. HF-SCDs@Cu exhibited the pronounced targeting propensity to tumor tissue and degraded to release ferrocene, Cu2+, and SCDs under the specific acidic tumor microenvironment, thereby eliciting potent tumor immunogenic cell death function induced by ferroptosis and PDT. Moreover, the O2-generating ability induced by HF-SCDs@Cu could reverse the hypoxic tumor microenvironment, enhancing the efficacy of PDT, and importantly, the expression of PD-L1 on tumor cell surfaces could be effectively downregulated by inhibiting the HIF-1α pathways, thereby augmenting the effect of anti-PD-L1 (αPD-L1) therapy. Therefore, this study presents a novel approach for the redevelopment of silkworm excrement and provides valuable strategies into enhancing PD-L1-mediated ICB therapy.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Immune checkpoint blockade (ICB) represents a novel approach to cancer treatment; however, the intricate tumor immune-suppressive microenvironment (TIME) significantly hinders the therapeutic efficacy [1,2]. At present, inducing immunogenic cell death (ICD) to enhance immunogenicity has emerged as an affirmed and crucial strategy for PD-L1-mediated ICB [3,4]. Especially, interferon-γ (IFN-γ) produced by activated T cells, which plays an important role as an inflammatory factor in immune response and inflammation [5,6]. However, in the meantime, IFN-γ can effectively regulate the upregulation of PD-L1 expression on the surface of tumor cells, which is not conducive to ICB effects [7,8]. Hence, relatively downregulating PD-L1 expression will be another valuable and effective strategy to enhance the efficacy of PD-L1-mediated ICB. The weak acidic and hypoxic environment of tumor induced by Warburg effect can inhibit the metabolic activity of CD8+ T cells and promote the functions of various immune-suppressive cells [9,10]. And importantly, the hypoxia-induced upregulation of HIF-1α can effectively stimulate the expression of PD-L1 [11,12], which is an overlooked yet crucial pathway to regulate the expression of PD-L1. Therefore, augmenting immunogenicity via ICD in conjunction with downregulating PD-L1 by reversing hypoxia will be a novel approach to augment the efficacy of PD-L1-mediated ICB therapy [13–16].

Recent studies have shown that ferroptosis induced by the Fenton or Fenton-like reaction of nanomaterials doped with transition metals can effectively reverse immune suppression in the tumor microenvironment [17,18]. The most salient characteristic of ferroptosis is the accumulation of lipid peroxidation (LPO), resulting in an imbalance in oxidative stress. In contrast to normal cells, tumor cells exhibit heightened susceptibility to oxidative stress due to their heavy reliance on antioxidant mechanisms, which is a consequence of the Warburg effect maintaining their metabolism [19,20]. Hence, reactive oxygen species (ROS)-mediated oxidative stress plays a pivotal role in promoting ferroptosis. The singlet oxygen (1O2) generated through photodynamic therapy (PDT) is an extremely oxidizing ROS species capable of effectively inducing oxidative stress within cells, thereby promoting ferroptosis and immune activation [21,22]. However, the efficacy of PDT is often hindered by the hypoxic microenvironment within tumors. Thus, addressing tumor hypoxia not only downregulates PD-L1 expression but also enhances PDT effectiveness. Fortunately, this challenge can be effectively overcome by the O2 generated through the Fenton or Fenton-like reaction. Hence, the rational design of nanomedicines that combine ferroptosis, PDT, and O2-generating capabilities for tumor immunotherapy represents a highly promising and innovative therapeutic approach.

In contrast to small molecule drugs, the unique size and precisely controlled composition, structure, and functional group modifications of nanomedicines confer enhanced adaptability to TIME [23–25]. Among these, carbon dots (CDs) have garnered extensive attention the their exceptional stability, favorable biocompatibility, facile functionalization, and distinctive optical properties [26–28]. Silkworm excrement of the insect Bombyx mori (Linnaeus) is currently primarily regarded as biological waste, with only a small portion utilized in traditional Chinese medicine to treat blood deficiency and rheumatic pain [29]. However, owing to the abundant presence of polyphenols, flavonoids, and chlorophyll in silkworm excrement, it may possess significant potential for anti-tumor therapy. To date, there have been no investigations into the utilization of silkworm excrement in the synthesis of CDs for tumor treatment. In this paper, we prepared a nanoparticle composite (HF-SCDs@Cu) with combined ferroptosis, PDT and O2-generating capabilities to remodel the tumor microenvironment and augment immunogenicity, thereby bolstering immunotherapy (Scheme 1 ). Firstly, we synthesized red-emitting silkworm excrement CDs (SCDs) through the “Top-down” method and complexed with copper ions to prepare Cu-doped SCDs (SCDs@Cu). And then, the preparation of HF-SCDs@Cu was achieved by encapsulating SCDs@Cu with hyaluronic acid-ferrocene (HA-Fc) micelles to confer tumor cell targeting capability. HF-SCDs@Cu effectively accumulated in tumor tissue following intravenous administration, where decomposed and released SCDs, Fc and Cu2+ within the acidic tumor microenvironment. In its capacity as a Fenton/Fenton-like reaction catalyst, Fe2+ and Cu+ effectively facilitated the conversion of H2O2 into ·OH and O2. Simultaneously, Fe3+ and Cu2+ could deplete cellular GSH levels and downregulate GPX4 protein expression, leading to a cascade of ROS-induced oxidative stress that induces lipid peroxidation and triggers ferroptosis. Withal, the positive feedback mechanisms of “laser-triggered PDT, O2-generation promote PDT, PDT accelerated LPO accumulation and ferroptosis,” effectively triggered ICD in tumor cells, significantly enhancing their immunogenicity. Importantly, HF-SCDs@Cu could effectively downregulate the expression of PD-L1 protein by inhibiting the HIF-1α pathway and enhance T cell infiltration, thereby significantly augmenting the efficacy of immune checkpoint inhibitors (αPD-L1). In summary, the utilization of HF-SCDs@Cu offers a novel approach for the redevelopment and exploitation of silkworm excrement, while this combined/self-enhanced strategy elucidates a new therapeutic regimen for tumor immunotherapy.

The target carbon dots SCDs@Cu were synthesized according to a “Top-down” method. Firstly, silkworm excrement was ground into a fine powder and dissolved in acidic ethanol. Filtered out the insoluble impurities, and then followed by extraction with water/dichloromethane to remove water-soluble salts. Subsequently, the organic layer product was dissolved in the alkaline ethanol solution and heated at 180°C for 6 h. Finally, the acquired nano products were mixed with a substantial quantity of CuCl2 solution, further purified via centrifugation and dialysis to yield SCDs@Cu. Displayed in Fig. 1
a, SCDs@Cu demonstrated the uniform and monodispersed morphology as observed by transmission electron microscopy (TEM), with an average particle size of approximately 4.2 nm. In the high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image (Fig. 1 a, inset), lattice fringes measuring 0.21 nm were visible, corresponding to the graphitized carbon (100) plane, a finding further supported by X-ray diffraction (XRD) peaks centered at 2θ = 23° (Fig. 1 b). The Raman spectrum of SCDs@Cu (Fig. 1 c) exhibited the G band corresponding to sp2-hybridized carbon and the D band associated with sp3-hybridized carbon, featuring an intensity ratio (ID/IG) of approximately 0.83, indicative of the highly ordered carbon core in SCDs@Cu.

In order to endow nanomedicines with tumor-targeting capability and based on the characteristic of ferrocene to induce ferroptosis [30,31], we have, for the first time, linked HA and Fc via Schiff base (C=N) in order to facilitate the responsiveness and rapid degradation in the tumor microenvironment [32,33]. Firstly, we conjugated the carboxyl group of HA with ethylenediamine through the EDC/NHS-activated amide synthesis method, and then the ferrocenecarboxaldehyde was linked via Schiff base to form a polymer system with both hydrophilic and hydrophobic ends, leading to the formation of HA-Fc micelles through self-assembly. By utilizing scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and TEM (Figs. 1 d and 1e), HA-Fc exhibited a uniform spherical structure, with an average particle size of approximately 62.3 nm. Building upon the successful preparation of SCDs@Cu and HA-Fc, we attempted to load SCDs@Cu into HA-Fc, and through multiple experiments, we observed that the addition of SCDs@Cu during the formation of HA-Fc resulted in the successful fabrication of HF-SCDs@Cu. Upon analysis using SEM and TEM (Figs. 1 f and 1 g), the particle size of HF-SCDs@Cu has expanded to approximately 82.4 nm. And in conjunction with the absorption spectrum (Fig. 1 h), it was evident that SCDs@Cu have been effectively incorporated into the HA-Fc micelles, with a calculated loading efficiency of 21.8%. Moreover, the Fourier Transform Infrared (FTIR) spectrum (Fig. 1 i) indicated that the peak at 1696 cm−1 corresponded to the C=O vibration, the peak at 1664 cm−1 to the C=N vibration of the Schiff base, the peak at 1612 cm−1 to the C=C vibration, and the peaks ranging from 1184 to 920 cm−1 were characteristic of polysaccharides. The chemical compositions of HF-SCDs@Cu were further elucidated through X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis (Fig. 1 j), the full-scan spectrum exhibited diffraction peaks corresponding to C 1s, N 1s, O 1s, Fe 2p, and Cu 2p, which were determined by peaks at 284.5, 399.5, 532.5, 724.1 and 934.5 eV, respectively. Among them, the high-resolution XPS analysis revealed the presence of Fe2+ through two characteristic peaks at 713.1 eV (Fe 2p3/2) and 725.0 eV (Fe 2p1/2) in the Fe 2p spectrum, as well as the presence of Cu2+ indicated by two characteristic peaks at 934.5 eV (Cu 2p3/2) and 954.4 eV (Cu 2p1/2) in the Cu 2p spectrum, thus confirming the successful preparation of HF-SCDs@Cu (Figs. 1 k and 1l). Due to the breakdown of Schiff base under acidic conditions, we simulated the weak acidic TIME using PBS solution (pH 5.5) and observed the disintegration of HF-SCDs@Cu through TEM. Under weak acidic conditions, as depicted in Fig. 1 m, HF-SCDs@Cu degraded and released SCDs. The lattice stripes of the SCDs revealed by HRTEM further confirmed the successful preparation and biodegradability of HF-SCDs@Cu. Furthermore, HF-SCDs@Cu displayed distinct fluorescent characteristics in diverse environments. As depicted in Figs. 1 n and 1o, the fluorescence quenching of HF-SCDs@Cu arose from the complexation with Cu2+; however, under weak acidic conditions, hydrogen protons disrupted the coordination bond between Cu2+ and surface functional groups, thereby restoring the quenched fluorescence ( Figs. S2-S5 ). This suggests that HF-SCDs@Cu represents a drug delivery system capable of responding to acidic stimulation and exhibits potential for fluorescent screening of tumor.

Owing to the acidic, hypoxic, high GSH and H2O2 expression of tumor microenvironment, it leads to heightened proliferation and inadequate immune infiltration [34–36]. Based on the sensitivity of HF-SCDs@Cu to weak acidic conditions and the ability to release Cu2+, Fc, and SCDs, which is expected to consume GSH, generate ·OH and O2 (Fig. 2
a). 5,5'-Dithiobis-(2-nitrobenzoic acid) (DTNB) is commonly employed as a probe for the detection of GSH. The underlying principle lies in the reduction of DTNB by GSH to yield 2-Nitro-5-mercaptobenzoic acid (TNB), leading to a decrease in original absorbance at 325 nm and the emergence of a new absorption peak at 412 nm ( Fig. S18 ) [37,38]. As depicted in Fig. 2 b, the ability of HF-SCDs@Cu to consume GSH was observed by simulating the weak acidity of tumor. It is evident that with increasing concentration of HF-SCDs@Cu, the absorbance at 412 nm gradually decreased, providing evidence for the GSH-depleting ability of HF-SCDs@Cu. However, the absorbance at 412 nm was significantly reduced only under acidic environment compared to neutral/alkaline environment, indicating that the acidic condition plays a crucial role in GSH consumption by HF-SCDs@Cu. Interestingly, after pretreatment with H2O2, the ability of HF-SCDs@Cu to consume GSH was enhanced, as the Fe2+ of Fc was oxidized by H2O2 to Fe3+, further depleting GSH (Fig. 2 c).

To determine the production of ·OH, we employed 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) as a probe for monitoring, which can be oxidized by ·OH to ox-TMB, with a characteristic absorption peak at 652 nm ( Fig. S19 ) [39,40]. As depicted in Figs. 2 d and 2e, the levels of ·OH produced by the Fe2+-initiating Fenton reaction exhibited a positive correlation with the reaction acidity and the concentration of HF-SCDs@Cu. After pretreatment with GSH, the capacity of HF-SCDs@Cu to produce ·OH was further augmented via the Cu+-mediated Fenton-like reaction. Hence, the cycling of metal ion valences served as a continuous energy source for GSH consumption and ·OH generation. The Fenton/Fenton-like reaction, initiated by low-valent transition metal ions, is a cyclic catalytic process that generates ·OH and O2. [Ru(dpp)3]Cl2 (RDPP) serves as a specific fluorescent probe for detecting O2, with the fluorescence intensity being significantly quenched by O2 [41]. Fig. 2 f illustrated that the combination of HF-SCDs@Cu and H2O2 results in a significant quenching of RDPP fluorescence compared to other conditions, indicating the strong O2 production capability of HF-SCDs@Cu.

Considering the near-infrared absorption and red-emitting properties under acidic conditions of HF-SCDs@Cu, we were inspired to further investigate the phototoxicity of HF-SCDs@Cu for PDT. 1O2 produced during PDT is one of the strong oxidizing ROS, which can be determined by SOSG fluorescence probe (Fig. 2 g). The main principle is that SOSG emits green fluorescence in the presence of 1O2 (λex = 504 nm, λem = 526 nm) [42,43]. As depicted in Fig. 2h, the fluorescence intensity of SOSG exhibited a significant increase after HF-SCDs@Cu with photoexcitation at 660 nm, indicating the capability to generate 1O2. To calculated the yield of 1O2, we utilized DPBF as a probe and MB as the relative reference standard. As depicted in Figs. 2i-2 l, S20, S21 and
Table S1 , the yield of 1O2 of HF-SCDs@Cu under alkaline conditions was reduced to 25.4%, indicating that the complexation of Cu2+ and encapsulation of HA-Fc have significantly diminished the 1O2 production of HF-SCDs@Cu, thereby impeding the PDT effect. However, under weak acidic conditions, HF-SCDs@Cu exhibited a 1O2 yield of 47.8%, attributed to the disintegration of HF-SCDs@Cu and the release of SCDs, which was further confirmed by the enhanced 1O2 yield of SCDs@Cu in the acidic environment. Hence, HF-SCDs@Cu exhibits significant potential for PDT.

Encouraged by the fact that HF-SCDs@Cu exhibited the ability of GSH consumption, ·OH and O2 production in the simulated tumor microenvironment, we further investigated these effects in CT26 cells. Due to the capacity of HF-SCDs@Cu to specifically target CD44 receptors on the surface of tumor cells and emit red fluorescence in the tumor microenvironment, we confirmed the cellular uptake ability by assessing the fluorescence intensity within the cells [44]. As depicted in Fig. 3
a, the cells displayed intense red fluorescence following a 3-hour treatment with HF-SCDs@Cu, as compared to SCDs@Cu, indicating the effective HA-mediated targeting ability. Furthermore, if the cells were pre-treated with HA to occupy the CD44 receptors, only a minimal amount of HF-SCDs@Cu penetrated into the cells. The distinct contrast demonstrates the outstanding tumor-targeting capability of HF-SCDs@Cu and the potential for tumor fluorescence diagnosis. To assess the cytotoxic effects of HF-SCDs@Cu, a standard CCK8 assay was performed on CT26 cancer cells and L929 normal cells. Fig. 3 b illustrates a notable decrease in the viability of CT26 cells after co-cultivation with HF-SCDs@Cu for 24 h, and a further significant reduction upon exposure to 660 nm laser irradiation, indicating the potent cytotoxic effect of 1O2 generated through photoexcitation of HF-SCDs@Cu. Moreover, a notable disparity in survival rates between normal cells and tumor cells is evident. Following treatment with HF-SCDs@Cu at concentrations of up to 100 μg mL−1, the viability of L929 cells remained consistently around 85%, while that of CT26 tumor cells was approximately 45% and decreased further to only 20% after PDT ( Figs. S23-S25 ). Overall, the HF-SCDs@Cu exhibited lower toxicity towards normal cells compared to CT26 cells, demonstrating specific cytotoxicity. After that, we further verified the cytotoxicity of HF-SCDs@Cu to CT26 cells through Calcein-AM/PI staining. As the dosage of HF-SCDs@Cu increased, the population of viable cells (green fluorescence) gradually decreased, while the number of dead cells (red fluorescence) increased. Upon increasing the dosage of HF-SCDs@Cu to 200 μg mL−1 and after laser irradiation, nearly all cells died, consistent with the findings from the CCK8 assay (Fig. 3 c).

To further explore the capacity of HF-SCDs@Cu to generate ROS in cells, 2′,7′-Dichlorofluorescein diacetate (DCFH-DA) is commonly used as a ROS probe, and the green fluorescence intensity is positively correlated with the level of ROS in cells [45]. As depicted in Fig. 3 d, the low-valent transition metal ions rapidly reacted with intracellular H2O2 to generate ·OH and the ROS level was further augmented by the 1O2 generated by HF-SCDs@Cu upon photoexcitation. Based on the aforementioned evidence of the strong consumption capacity of high-valent transition metal ions for GSH, we quantified the relative content of intracellular GSH using a GSH assay kit (Fig. 3 e). At a concentration of 100 μg mL−1, approximately 45% of intracellular GSH has been depleted. However, upon exposure to additional 660 nm laser irradiation, around 60% of GSH can be consumed. It can be attributed to the fact that the generated 1O2 further diminished the GSH levels.

The increased intracellular oxidative stress, caused by higher levels of ROS and lower levels of GSH, was a theoretical basis for the effective induction of ferroptosis. Ferroptosis is frequently accompanied by mitochondrial dysfunction, characterized by a significant reduction in mitochondrial membrane potential (MMP), which serves as a pivotal indicator.[46–48] Here, JC-1 was used as a probe to measure MMP, as shown in Fig. 4
a, the healthy mitochondria exhibited red fluorescence, whereas upon treatment with HF-SCDs@Cu, the cells demonstrated a reduction in red fluorescence intensity accompanied by an increase in green fluorescence signal. Furthermore, this effect was further potentiated upon exposure to laser irradiation, indicating that HF-SCDs@Cu combined PDT could significantly induce mitochondrial damage. Then, the occurrence of ferroptosis was further verified by quantifying the intracellular relative levels of LPO using the MDA detection kit. The CT26 cells were subjected to individual treatments with PBS, HF-SCDs@Cu, HF-SCDs@Cu + Laser, HF-SCDs@Cu + Fer-1 (Ferrostatin-1), HF-SCDs@Cu + Laser + Fer-1, respectively. As shown in Fig. 4 b, compared to the control group, the LPO significantly increased in HF-SCDs@Cu treatment and was further enhanced by laser irradiation, which can be attributed to the effective enhancement of oxidative stress by the 1O2 during PDT. Importantly, no significant increase in LPO was observed after additional treatment with Fer-1 (a classical ferroptosis inhibitor) [49], indicating that Fer-1 inhibited the occurrence of ferroptosis induced by HF-SCDs@Cu and suggesting that HF-SCDs@Cu could effectively mediate ferroptosis in CT26 cells. LPO can also be detected by using C11-BODIPY as a luorescent indicator. Upon C11-BODIPY being oxidized, its fluorescence will change from red to green, thus facilitating the membrane LPO detection. Predictably, the cells treated with HF-SCDs@Cu combined with PDT exhibited brighter green fluorescence and darker red fluorescence (Fig. 4 c), indicating a relatively high level of lipid peroxidation. GSH-GPX4 pathway plays a crucial role in inhibiting ferroptosis by defending against oxidative stress.[50,51] As another marker of ferroptosis, the expression level of GPX4 can effectively reflect the detoxification effect on LPO. In this paper, the expression of GPX4 was investigated by immunofluorescence assay, as depicted in Fig. 4 d, the level of GPX4 was significantly reduced in CT26 cells treated with HF-SCDs@Cu and further decreased under additional laser irradiation, which can be attributed to the effective reduction of GPX4 expression through the consumption of GSH by HF-SCDs@Cu. Therefore, in conclusion, HF-SCDs@Cu effectively circumvented the ferroptosis defense mechanism and elicited substantial ROS production, thereby robustly promoting ferroptosis.

Recent studies have demonstrated that ferroptosis and PDT can effectively induce ICD, characterized by the release of damage-associated molecular patterns (DAMPs), mainly including calreticulin (CRT), high mobility group box 1 (HMGB1) and adenosine triphosphate (ATP) [52,53]. As an “eat me” signaling protein, CRT can undergo transport to the cell membrane for recognition and binding by dendritic cells during the early stages of ICD. In this study, immunofluorescence staining was used to reflect the CRT on the surface of cell membrane. As shown in Fig. 5
a, compared with other treatment groups, the extracellular red fluorescence was the strongest after HF-SCDs@Cu combined with PDT treatment, indicating that a large amount of CRT was transferred to the extracellular membrane. As an inflammatory cytokine, HMGB1 predominantly localizes within the nucleus; however, during the intermediate and late stages of ICD, HMGB1 is released from the nucleus to initiate the activation of inflammatory responses. As depicted in Fig. 5 b, HMGB1 exhibited prominent nuclear localization in both the PBS group and PBS + Laser group. However, treatment with HF-SCDs@Cu and additional laser irradiation resulted in the enhanced release of HMGB1. Furthermore, analysis of HMGB1 content in the culture supernatant revealed significantly higher levels in the HF-SCDs@Cu and HF-SCDs@Cu + Laser groups, indicating that HF-SCDs@Cu combined with PDT treatment could synergistically promote HMGB1 release from tumor cells (Fig. 5 c). Moreover, ATP functions as a “find me” signaling that can promote infiltration of effector T cells, which can be released during the intermediate and early stages of ICD. As shown in Fig. 5 d, the secretion of ATP was significantly higher in the HF-SCDs@Cu group compared to the PBS group and PBS + Laser group, with even more pronounced effects when combined with PDT. In summary, HF-SCDs@Cu combined with PDT can effectively induce ICD by promoting CRT exposure, HMGB1 release, and ATP secretion, thereby activating the immune response.

ce:section.ce:section-title: HF-SCDs@Cu decreased HIF-1α and PD-L1 levels
ce:section.ce:para: The hypoxic microenvironment of tumors, as a significant immune-related adverse event, confers upon tumors the capacity to promote metastasis and drug resistance, particularly by upregulating HIF-1α expression induced by hypoxia. What has been determined so far is that in the hypoxic microenvironment of tumor tissues, the accumulated HIF-1α migrates into the nucleus and combines with the HIF-1β subunit to form a dimer, which then binds to the hypoxia response element in the promoter region of the PD-L1 gene to initiate the transcription process, thereby resulting in increased PD-L1 expression [54–56]. Thus, effectively suppressing the expression of HIF-1α offers the potential for selectively inhibiting the expression of PD-L1 [57]. Based on the O2-generating ability of HF-SCDs@Cu, we assessed the expression of HIF-1α through immunofluorescence staining. As depicted in Fig. 5 e, compared to the control group, a noticeable reduction in HIF-1α expression was observed after treatment with HF-SCDs@Cu, which indicates that the HF-SCDs@Cu-mediated O2 generation could effectively attenuate cellular HIF-1α expression. Based on the co-expression characteristics of HIF-1α and PD-L1, we employed immunofluorescence staining to track the expression levels of PD-L1. Encouragingly, the trend of PD-L1 expression was consistent with that of HIF-1α (Fig. 5 f). Consequently, the co-inhibition of HIF-1α and PD-L1 expression by HF-SCDs@Cu endows with powerful immunotherapeutic potential and provides the robust support for the effectiveness of ICB therapy.

To establish the optimal experimental protocol for evaluating the HF-SCDs@Cu combined PDT anti-tumor effect in vivo, we employed a BALB/c mouse model to determine the initiation of PDT by monitoring the accumulation of HF-SCDs@Cu within tumors. As illustrated in Fig. 6
a, HF-SCDs@Cu started to accumulate at the tumor site after intravenous administration and the highest level of enrichment was observed at 8 h. After that, the metabolic-dominant mode of action commenced and was nearly fully metabolized within 48 h. Subsequently, tissue samples from the liver, heart, lung, kidney, spleen, and tumor were obtained through dissection of mice treated for 8 h. Fluorescence imaging reveals a significantly stronger fluorescence signal in the tumor tissue compared to other organs, indicating potential accumulation of HF-SCDs@Cu at the tumor tissue site via HA-targeting after intravenous injection for 8 h, thus establishing the initiation time for PDT synergistic therapy.

Given the promising anti-tumor effects observed in vitro with the HF-SCDs@Cu combined PDT, we established the subcutaneous CT26 tumor BALB/c mouse model to assess the therapeutic efficacy in vivo. Upon reaching a tumor volume of 120 mm3, the mice were randomly allocated into five groups and treated with saline (Control), SCDs@Cu, HF-SCDs@Cu, SCDs@Cu + Laser and HF-SCDs@Cu + Laser, respectively. As shown in the treatment plan (Fig. 6 b), systematic therapy was administered at three-day intervals, with tumor volume changes assessed every two days. During the course of treatment, it was observed that the tumor growth rate in the control group of mice was remarkably rapid, with a volume reaching 15 times its initial size. Notably, mice treated with HF-SCDs@Cu exhibited a notable inhibitory effect on tumor growth. Furthermore, when combined with PDT, there was a strong suppression of tumor growth in mice, consistent with the findings from in vitro cell experiments (Figs. 6 c-6e). After 14 days of treatment, we collected mouse blood for biochemical analysis, which primarily included the assessment of alanine aminotransferase (ALT), aspartate aminotransferase (AST), creatinine (CREA), blood urea nitrogen (BUN), and red blood cell counts (RBC). The results of hematological and biochemical parameters in mice reveals no significant abnormalities between the experimental groups, indicating that HF-SCDs@Cu exhibited favorable biocompatibility and was suitable for intravenous administration (Fig. 6 f). Throughout the entire treatment period, no significant changes in the body weight of the mice were observed ( Fig. S30 ), indicating that the therapeutic regimen employed exhibited minimal negative effects on their overall health. The hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) staining of the various organ revealed no significant damage to the organ tissues in each treatment group ( Fig. S31 ). However, tumors subjected to HF-SCDs@Cu combined PDT exhibited substantial tissue damage, indicating that HF-SCDs@Cu significantly inhibits tumor proliferation (Fig. 6 g). Given the in vitro experimental findings demonstrating the effective activation of ferroptosis and ICD by HF-SCDs@Cu, we proceeded to assess its in vivo effects through immunofluorescence staining of tumor tissues. As depicted in Fig. 6 g, HF-SCDs@Cu significantly reduced the expression of GPX4 proteins, and the result of CRT was also consistent with the observed trends in the in vitro experiment, suggesting that the treatment of HF-SCDs@Cu combined PDT could effectively trigger ICD and demonstrate potential for immunotherapy with this treatment approach.

To further investigate the levels of immune activation facilitated by HF-SCDs@Cu, we conducted immunofluorescence analysis to detect interleukin-2 (IL-2), a cytokine that promotes T cell differentiation [58,59]. The results revealed heightened expression of IL-2 in tumors treated with HF-SCDs@Cu combined PDT, indicating a significant enhancement in lymphocyte growth, proliferation, and differentiation at this stage. CD86 serves as a robust indicator of mature dendritic cells (DCs), with its increased expression signifying the effective activation of DC maturation by HF-SCDs@Cu combined PDT, thereby resulting in efficient T cell activation [60,61]. Notably, CD8+ T cells, as pivotal T lymphocytes, serve as essential indicators for evaluating the efficacy of immunotherapy. The immunofluorescence detection results indicated that the HF-SCDs@Cu + Laser group exhibited elevated expression levels of CD8, suggesting that T lymphocytes were in the “positive” state and indicating that the immune response has been activated by HF-SCDs@Cu combined PDT. The cytotoxic activity of CD8+ T cells is largely reliant on the production of IFN-γ [62]. Here, we conducted an ELISA assay to measure the levels of IFN-γ, and the results revealed a significantly higher secretion of IFN-γ in the HF-SCDs@Cu + Laser group compared to others, consistent with the aforementioned findings (Fig. 6 h). Furthermore, the released IFN-γ can induce inflammatory responses, leading to polarization of tumor-associated macrophages (TAMs) towards the anti-tumor M1 phenotype, thereby facilitating infiltration of CD8+ T cells and restricting tumor immune evasion.[63] Arginase 1 (ARG 1), a crucial indicator of immunosuppressive M2-type TAMs, the expression was notably diminished in the HF-SCDs@Cu + Laser group, suggesting a conversion of M2-type TAMs to M1-type. Moreover, we investigated the survival outcomes of mice, as illustrated in Fig. 6 i, where all mice in the control group succumbed within 28 days. Conversely, a majority of mice in the HF-SCDs@Cu + Laser group exhibited extended survival beyond 42 days, indicating that HF-SCDs@Cu combined PDT significantly extended the lifespan of mice and provided compelling evidence for in vivo anti-tumor efficacy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: HF-SCDs@Cu induced PD-L1 downregulation in vivo
ce:section.ce:para:
In vitro experiments, we validated that HF-SCDs@Cu could significantly induce the downregulation of PD-L1 expression on the cell surface. However, while a similar trend was observed in vivo, the extent of downregulation was relatively diminished, possibly attributable to the upregulation of PD-L1 by a substantial amount of IFN-γ induced through immune activation (Fig. 7
a) [7,8]. To examine this hypothesis, we established CT26 bilateral tumor bearing mice and divided into 4 treatment groups: Saline, Saline + anti-IFN-γ, HF-SCDs@Cu, and HF-SCDs@Cu + anti-IFN-γ (Fig. 7 b). Here, the neutralizing antibody (anti-IFN-γ) was used to counteract the abundant IFN-γ in vivo, and the tumor on the right side was irradiated with laser (660 nm, 50 mW cm−2). Following three treatment cycles, the mice were euthanized and the tumors were subjected to HIF-1α and PD-L1 immunofluorescence staining. As shown in Fig. 7 c, compared to the Saline group, the expression of HIF-1α was significantly reduced after treatment with HF-SCDs@Cu, but there was not a strong suppression of PD-L1, particularly when combined with PDT. However, in comparison to HF-SCDs@Cu and Saline + anti-IFN-γ groups, HF-SCDs@Cu + anti-IFN-γ group exhibited significant suppression of PD-L1 expression. It can be inferred that the immune activation triggered by traditional ICD leads to the release of IFN-γ, which effectively enhances the expression of PD-L1. Excitingly, HF-SCDs@Cu not only effectively activate the immune system but also efficiently suppress the expression of HIF-1α by O2 generation, thereby relatively inhibiting the expression of PD-L1. Therefore, HF-SCDs@Cu represents a highly suitable nanomedicine for PD-L1-mediated ICB therapy.

To further confirm the functionality of the anti-tumor immune response induced by HF-SCDs@Cu combined PDT, we investigated the synergistic effect of combined αPD-L1 in vivo by establishing the dual tumor mouse model. The tumor on the right side was treated with intra-tumoral injection as the primary lesion, and the left side was treated as the distant metastasis. The treatment method is illustrated in Fig. 8
a. Upon reaching a tumor volume of 120 mm3, CT26 bilateral tumor bearing mice were randomly divided into 5 groups, which were treated with saline, αPD-L1, HF-SCDs@Cu, HF-SCDs@Cu + Laser, and HF-SCDs@Cu + Laser + αPD-L1, respectively. The therapeutic status of the tumor was illustrated in Figs. 8 b-8e, the monotherapy of αPD-L1 failed to exhibit significant tumor suppression, which was limited by insufficient immunogenicity and inadequate immune infiltration. However, the HF-SCDs@Cu + Laser and HF-SCDs@Cu + Laser + αPD-L1 groups exhibited a highly significant inhibition of tumor proliferation in the primary tumor. For distant tumors, we observed that the HF-SCDs@Cu + Laser group demonstrated ideal tumor growth inhibition compared to the control group. Excitingly, the HF-SCDs@Cu + Laser + αPD-L1 group demonstrated a remarkably significant inhibition of tumor growth, which was due to the effective activation of immunogenicity by HF-SCDs@Cu combined PDT, and the relatively reduction of PD-L1 expression on the surface of tumor cells, further enhancing the efficacy of αPD-L1. Moreover, there was no significant change in the body weight of mice observed during the treatment period (Fig. 8 f), and histological examination using H&E staining ( Fig. S32 ) revealed uniform organ density with no apparent damage across all experimental groups. In addition, we further analyzed the proportions of CD4+ T cells and CD8+ T cells in the spleen and tumor of mice by flow cytometry to reflect the degree of immune activation. As illustrated in Fig. 8 g, the HF-SCDs@Cu + Laser + αPD-L1 group exhibited significantly elevated levels of both CD4+ T cells and CD8+ T cells in distant tumors (12.61% and 8.52%, respectively), in stark contrast to the baseline levels observed in the Saline-treated group (1.98% and 1.27%, respectively). For spleen, the levels of CD4+ T cells and CD8+ T cells in the HF-SCDs@Cu + Laser + αPD-L1 group (20.92% and 14.25%, respectively) are substantially higher than those in the Saline-treated group (6.28% and 4.52%, respectively). These promising findings validate the efficacy of HF-SCDs@Cu combined PDT in stimulating anti-tumor immune responses, while also providing compelling evidence for the safety and efficacy of combined PD-L1-mediated ICB therapy as a viable strategy for cancer treatment.

In summary, we have developed a smart TME/NIR stimulus responsive nanoagent, HF-SCDs@Cu, for efficient synergistic tumor therapy by ferroptosis, PDT and αPD-L1. HF-SCDs@Cu rapidly disintegrated in response to TME, releasing Cu2+, ferrocene and SCDs. The rapid consumption of GSH by Fe3+ and Cu2+ further inhibited the expression of GPX4, while Fe2+ and Cu+ initiated the Fenton/Fenton-like reactions that significantly augmented the intracellular ROS level and generate substantial amounts of LPO, thereby inducing ferroptosis. Simultaneously, the O2-generating capacity of HF-SCDs@Cu can effectively mitigate the alleviate the hypoxia limitation of PDT. Moreover, HF-SCDs@Cu-induced ferroptosis/PDT can effectively trigger ICD, thereby addressing the issues of inadequate immunogenicity and insufficient immune infiltration. Importantly, the O2-generating ability induced by HF-SCDs@Cu effectively downregulates the expression of PD-L1 on tumor cell surfaces by inhibiting the HIF-1α pathways, thereby augmenting the αPD-L1 therapeutic effect, which proves that HF-SCDs@Cu is a potential nanomedicine that is suitable for PD-L1-mediated ICB therapy.

The supplementary data include FTIR spectrum of Fc, HA and HA-Fc, fluorescence emission spectra of HF-SCDs@Cu and SCDs@Cu in PBS (pH 5.5/7.4), XPS spectra of HF-SCDs@Cu and SCDs@Cu, the detection mechanism of DTNB and TMB, cell viability of L929/CT26 cells by CCK8 assay, body weight of BALB/c mice changes, H&E staining of heart, liver, spleen, lung and kidney, experimental data of 1O2 quantum yield and degradation of DPBF.
